does: This paper introduces SAE-Rad, a system that generates radiology reports from chest X-rays by decomposing vision transformer representations into human-interpretable features using sparse autoencoders. The approach extracts learned features from a pre-trained ViT, uses SAEs to identify sparse, interpretable components, then distills ground-truth reports into textual descriptions for each feature via an off-the-shelf language model. These feature descriptions are compiled into complete reports without requiring fine-tuning of large models. The system achieves competitive performance on the MIMIC-CXR dataset while maintaining interpretability, addressing hallucination and transparency issues present in standard vision-language models.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability program, particularly sparse autoencoders developed for understanding neuron-level representations in large language models, extending these techniques to vision-language tasks. It responds to growing concerns about hallucinations and lack of interpretability in clinical applications of vision-language models, which increasingly automate medical documentation. The approach leverages pre-trained vision transformers and language models rather than training end-to-end systems, following the trend of leveraging foundation models without expensive task-specific fine-tuning. The paper combines recent SAE architectural advances with the domain-specific challenge of automated radiology report generation, positioning interpretability not as an analysis tool but as a mechanism for downstream task performance.
enables: This work opens pathways for deploying AI systems in high-stakes medical settings where interpretability and auditability are regulatory and clinical requirements. By demonstrating that mechanistic interpretability techniques can drive downstream task performance, it suggests broader applications for SAEs beyond model analysis in multimodal reasoning tasks. The approach could extend to other medical imaging domains or clinical documentation tasks requiring trustworthy automation. It raises possibilities for interactive medical AI systems where individual features correspond to radiologically meaningful concepts, enabling clinicians to verify and correct system reasoning. Future work could explore whether SAE-discovered features align with established radiological terminology, enable more granular error analysis, or support few-shot adaptation to new clinical scenarios.
